//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_wsl}

NOTE: Workload identity inhabits *three namespaces*: a Windows local
user (auth boundary discovered by Windows OpenSSH), the workload's
*own* registered WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (populated in
workload's `HKCU\Lxss` via `wsl --import` from a seed tarball), and a
Linux user inside that workload-owned distribution (execution context
entered by the locked-down `command=` directive's `wsl.exe --user`).
The third namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design --
distributions register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`;
admin's distributions are invisible to workload sessions.

NOTE: Auth is decoupled from workload-profile state via an
admin-owned absolute-path authorized_keys.
{busn_caparison}
installs `Match User ${BUJB_workload_user}` in `sshd_config` routing
`AuthorizedKeysFile` to
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`.
Sshd's trust-file lookup never resolves the workload's `$HOME`.

NOTE: Workload provisioning runs as *four* atomic SSH-as-workload
sessions (step 11), each carrying a single cmdlet or native-binary
call per WSG WSp-105. The fourth session is `wsl.exe --shutdown`
alone, releasing the `NTUSER.DAT` hive before Windows unloads that
logon. Best-effort minimum-session-count: WSG-strict single-cmdlet
bodies and single-session-with-shutdown are mutually exclusive in
the transport stack, so multi-session demotion risk during sessions
1-3 is accepted; Layer 1 (caparison-installed absolute-path Match
block + admin-owned authorized_keys) is the auth defense if
demotion occurs. See Rationale.

NOTE: Cleanup (steps 4-8) is best-effort. Windows User Profile
Service performs asynchronous internal bookkeeping after profile
removal, so a single-pass 100% pristine state is not always
achievable; discipline is sufficient sweep that step 9's
`New-LocalUser` lands on a clean SID with the canonical profile
path.

NOTE: Garrison-w does not self-validate the locked-down round-trip.
The operator's first workload-SSH invocation post-garrison
(`buw-jwk`, `buw-jwc`, `buw-jws`) IS the round-trip validation; an
explicit invigilate fact for this assertion was retired as
overdesign once those operational tabtargets existed.

The
{busn_garrison}
operation on Windows via WSL:

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A
{burp_regime}
profile exists at the given
{burp_investiture}
and its
{burn_regime}
targets a Windows OpenSSH node, reachable as
{burp_privileged_user}
under key-only auth using
{burp_privileged_key_file}.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_caparison}
has run for this
{burp_investiture}
-- admin SSH trust established, `rbtww-main` staged under the admin
user at `C:\\bujb-wsl\\rbtww-main`, sshd_config carries the `Match
User ${BUJB_workload_user}` block routing `AuthorizedKeysFile` to
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
(parent directory pre-provisioned with admins/SYSTEM FullControl,
no workload grant -- sshd reads as SYSTEM, parallel to
`administrators_authorized_keys`).

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file}
is unencrypted and exists on the operator's station.

//axhos_step
. *Assert admin posture*:
.. {busc_call}
{bust_invigilate_windows}
for this
{burp_investiture}
.. {busc_store}
`«INVIGILATE_RESULT»`
.. {busc_require}
`«INVIGILATE_RESULT»` is zero
.. {busc_fatal}
on non-zero --
{bust_handbook_windows}
scope (operator-managed) or
{bust_caparison_windows}
scope (caparison-managed); diagnostic surfaced verbatim

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH session*:
.. {busc_call}
ssh as
{burp_privileged_user}
with
{burp_privileged_key_file};
key-auth only
.. {busc_store}
`«ADMIN_SESSION»`
.. {busc_fatal}
on connection failure --
{bust_caparison_windows}
scope

//axhos_step
. *Export admin's rbtww-main as workload-readable seed*:
.. {busc_call}
`wsl.exe --export rbtww-main C:\\bujb-wsl\\rbtww-seed.tar` over
`«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- writes the seed at the
{busn_caparison}-provisioned `C:\\bujb-wsl\\` directory whose default
inheritance from `C:\\` grants `BUILTIN\\Users:ReadAndExecute`,
making the seed readable by the workload SSH session in step 11.
Writing under the workload's Windows profile fails because admin's
`wsl --export` inherits admin's logon-SID ACL on the resulting file,
excluding the workload user
.. {busc_store}
`«SEED_TARBALL»` = `C:\\bujb-wsl\\rbtww-seed.tar`
.. {busc_fatal}
on export failure --
{bust_caparison_windows}
scope

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate Phase 1 -- Release hives*:
.. {busc_call}
`wsl.exe --shutdown` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- host-wide WSL VM
teardown
.. {busc_call}
`Stop-Process -Name wslhost,wslservice -Force -ErrorAction
SilentlyContinue` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- releases helper
processes that survive shutdown
.. {busc_fatal}
on either call's failure --
{bust_handbook_windows}
scope

//axhos_step

.. {busc_call}
`Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_UserProfile -Filter "LocalPath =
'C:\\Users\\${BUJB_workload_user}' OR LocalPath LIKE
'C:\\Users\\${BUJB_workload_user}.%'"` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` --
exact-match for canonical path + LIKE for numbered fallback dirs
from prior demotions; the unbounded `<user>%` form is forbidden
because it would prefix-match unrelated accounts like
`${BUJB_workload_user}_alt`. Emits one row per matching profile
.. For each row, {busc_call}
`Remove-CimInstance -Query "SELECT * FROM Win32_UserProfile WHERE
LocalPath = '<row.LocalPath>'"` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- one SSH
call per row per WSG WSp-105; User Profile Service handles
ProfileList SID key removal (canonical AND `.bak` variants),
`NTUSER.DAT` hive unload, and folder deletion atomically
.. {busc_fatal}
on probe or per-row remove failure --
{bust_handbook_windows}
scope

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate Phase 3 -- SAM scrub*:
.. {busc_call}
`Remove-LocalUser -Name '${BUJB_workload_user}' -ErrorAction
SilentlyContinue` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- collapsed dispatch per
the WSG WSp-108 nuclear-cleanup carve-out: narrow failure spectrum
at this callsite (admin context, SAM database, fixed workload user
name); step 9's `New-LocalUser` provides the downstream verification
by erroring on "account already exists" if a silent partial cleanup
left a SID behind
.. {busc_fatal}
on SSH transport failure --
{bust_handbook_windows}
scope

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate Phase 4 -- Filesystem sweep*:
.. {busc_call}
`Get-ChildItem -Path 'C:\\Users' -Filter '${BUJB_workload_user}*'
-Directory -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` --
dynamic discovery; emits zero or more directory rows that survived
Phase 2 (canonical and any numbered fallback dirs)
.. For each row, {busc_call}
`Remove-Item -Recurse -Force -LiteralPath '<row.FullName>'` over
`«ADMIN_SESSION»`
.. {busc_call}
`Test-Path 'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${BUJB_workload_user}'` over
`«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- non-destructive probe; returns state
.. {busc_store}
`«CYGWIN_HOME_PRESENT»` (`True` or `False`)
.. On `«CYGWIN_HOME_PRESENT»` equal to `True`, {busc_call}
`Remove-Item -Recurse -Force
'C:\\cygwin64\\home\\${BUJB_workload_user}'` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
-- probe-then-act per WSp-108 main rule; the nuclear-cleanup
carve-out does not qualify for arbitrary filesystem paths (NTFS ACL
friction, junction loops, file-locked-by-process expand the failure
spectrum beyond the carve-out's narrow-spectrum condition)
.. {busc_fatal}
on Windows-side probe or per-row remove failure --
{bust_handbook_windows}
scope

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate Phase 5 -- WSL vestige*:
.. {busc_call}
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root userdel
${BUJB_workload_user}` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- absent-state
(userdel exit code 6) is acceptable and treated as no-op; user
lives inside admin's `rbtww-main` as vestige from prior garrison
shapes
.. {busc_call}
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root rm -rf
/home/${BUJB_workload_user}` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- always-run
cleanup of orphan home from any prior partial garrison
.. {busc_fatal}
on userdel exit code other than 0 or 6, or on `rm -rf` failure --
{bust_handbook_windows}
scope

//axhos_step
. *Create Windows workload account*:
.. {busc_call}
`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
${BUJB_workload_user}` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` -- creates
{bujb_workload_user}
in the SAM database
(`HKLM\\SAM\\SAM\\Domains\\Account\\Users`). The
`HKLM\\...\\ProfileList\\<SID>` entry is *not* written by
`net.exe user /add`; User Profile Service populates it on the first

.. No Linux user is created inside admin's `rbtww-main` -- the
workload's Linux user is minted inside the workload's *own*
imported distribution in step 11
.. {busc_fatal}
on account-management failure

//axhos_step
. *Place bare workload trust*:
.. {busc_call}
`ssh-keygen -y` on
{burp_workload_key_file}
.. {busc_store}
`«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` from stdout
.. {busc_call}
`Set-Content` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` writing `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` as
a bare entry (no `command=` directive) to
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
-- single cmdlet per WSG WSp-105; admin-owned file throughout its
lifetime; directory pre-provisioned by
{busn_caparison}
with admins/SYSTEM FullControl only (sshd reads as SYSTEM, parallel

.. {busc_fatal}
on key-write failure

//axhos_step
. *Provision workload's WSL (four atomic SSH-as-workload sessions per WSG WSp-105)*:
.. {busc_call}
*Session 1 (import)*: ssh as
{bujb_workload_user}
with
{burp_workload_key_file}
running `wsl.exe --import rbtww-main <workload-wsl-root>
«SEED_TARBALL»` -- registers the workload's own `rbtww-main` in
workload's `HKCU\\Lxss`; `<workload-wsl-root>` is a workload-writable
directory inside the workload's Windows profile. The bare entry at

.. {busc_call}
*Session 2 (useradd)*: ssh as
{bujb_workload_user}
running `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd

inner Linux workload user. `useradd` produces a locked-by-default

.. {busc_call}
*Session 3 (privkey)*: ssh as
{bujb_workload_user}
running `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root install -D
-m 600 -o ${BUJB_workload_user} -g ${BUJB_workload_user} /dev/stdin
/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519`, redirecting
{burp_workload_key_file}
to ssh's stdin -- the key flows through ssh FD 0 -> cmd.exe -> wsl.exe
-> Linux child, where `install -D /dev/stdin` sets mode and owner
atomically and creates the parent `.ssh/` directory. No plaintext
key on either side's disk, no remote temp file, no key material
in argv
.. {busc_call}
*Session 4 (shutdown)*: ssh as
{bujb_workload_user}
running `wsl.exe --shutdown` -- releases the WSL VM and helper-
process hive handles (wslhost.exe, wslservice) before this session's
Windows logon unloads. Best-effort defense against demotion
accumulation across the four sessions; Layer 1
({busn_caparison}-installed absolute-path Match block) is the auth
defense if a stray demotion occurs
.. {busc_call}
delete `«SEED_TARBALL»` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
.. {busc_fatal}
on any session's failure -- workload state partially provisioned
across namespaces; revalidate from a clean garrison

//axhos_step
. *Lock down workload trust (admin rewrite)*:
.. {busc_call}
`Set-Content` over `«ADMIN_SESSION»` overwriting the absolute-path
authorized_keys at
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
with the locked-down line: directive prefix + `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»`
-- single cmdlet per WSG WSp-105; admin-owned file throughout; no
in-session rewrite race
.. Canonical directive prefix: `BUJB_command_w` in
{bujb_jurisdiction}
with `${BUJB_workload_user}` substituted at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----
.. {busc_fatal}
on rewrite failure -- bare trust still in place; subsequent
workload SSH would bypass the locked-down route; revalidate from a
clean garrison

//axhog_guarantee
On success:

* Exactly one workload identity named
{bujb_workload_user}
exists, mirrored across all three namespaces.
* Windows local user with SID registered under
`HKLM\\...\\ProfileList` (auth boundary).
* Workload-owned `rbtww-main` registered in workload's `HKCU\\Lxss`
(distribution namespace).
* WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context entered
by the locked-down `command=` directive's `wsl.exe --user`).
* Workload authorized_keys at
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/${BUJB_workload_user}/authorized_keys`
carries `«WORKLOAD_PUBKEY»` with the locked-down `command=`
directive -- admin-owned absolute path; auth decoupled from
workload `$HOME` via caparison-installed `Match User` block.
* {burp_workload_key_file}
planted at `/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` inside the
workload's distribution.
* `wsl.exe --shutdown` executed inside the workload session before
exit; no orphan `NTUSER.DAT` hive handles outlive garrison-w.
* Admin posture unchanged -- no `sshd_config` writes, no admin
authorized_keys writes.

Garrison-w does not self-validate the locked-down round-trip;
{bust_invigilate_windows}
asserts it as a post-completion fact.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when step 12's admin-context lockdown rewrite completes
after step 11's `wsl.exe --shutdown` and seed cleanup. Non-zero on
any step's
{busc_fatal}.

==== Rationale

*Four-session shape with final shutdown (step 11)*::
WSG WSp-105 (one cmdlet or native binary per body) forbids the
compound session shape (wsl --import + useradd + install-D + wsl
--shutdown all on one workload SSH connection). Each cmdlet
therefore opens its own session. Across sessions 1-3, helper
processes (wslhost.exe, wslservice) spawned by `wsl --import`
outlive their parent session and lock the NTUSER.DAT hive of the
workload's logon, exposing the demotion path (User Profile Service
relocating the canonical profile to `bujuw_user.<host>.NNN` on the
next logon). Session 4 is `wsl.exe --shutdown` as its only act,
releasing the WSL VM and the helper-process hive handles before
that session's logon unloads -- best-effort cleanup that limits but
does not eliminate the demotion risk across sessions 1-3. Layer 1
({busn_caparison}-installed absolute-path Match block + admin-owned
authorized_keys) is the auth defense if demotion still occurs:
sshd reads as SYSTEM from the absolute path and never resolves the
workload's `$HOME`. The lockdown rewrite (step 12) runs in admin
context after session 4 exits -- no in-session rewrite race.

*Two LIKE patterns in Phase 2*::
The query matches `LocalPath LIKE
'C:\Users\${BUJB_workload_user}%'` AND `LocalPath LIKE
'C:\Users\${BUJB_workload_user}.%'`. The first catches the
canonical path; the second catches numbered fallback dirs from
prior demotions (`bujuw_user.<host>.NNN`). One pattern alone leaks
state.

*Per-row Remove-CimInstance in Phase 2*::
WSG WSp-105 mandates one cmdlet per SSH body. The probe emits
rows; bash iterates and issues one Remove-CimInstance per row with
a LocalPath-specific `-Query`. A piped pattern (probe |
remove-CimInstance) would violate WSp-105 and is forbidden.

*Phase 5 is admin's rbtww-main, not workload's*::
Workload's distribution lives in workload's own `HKCU\Lxss`,
populated in step 11. Phase 5 cleans the workload's Linux user
vestige inside *admin's* `rbtww-main` -- vestige from prior
garrison shapes when workload shared admin's distribution. The
phase is retained because residual entries leak shell history and
HKCU pollution across garrisons.

==== References

* link:https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/administration/openssh/openssh_server_configuration[Microsoft Learn -- OpenSSH Server Configuration for Windows] (AuthorizedKeysFile, Match blocks)
* link:https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1383[Win32-OpenSSH #1383] -- local-user SID registration under ProfileList required for OpenSSH logon
* link:https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/user-profiles-and-logon/profile-loading-fails[Microsoft Learn -- Profile loading fails]
* link:https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/setup-upgrade-and-drivers/scripts-clean-profile-folder-prevent-temp-profile[Microsoft Learn -- Scripts to clean profile folder and prevent TEMP user profile creation]
* link:https://helgeklein.com/free-tools/delprof2-user-profile-deletion-tool/[helgeklein.com -- Delprof2] -- documented authority for safe profile teardown sequencing
* WSG WSp-105 -- one cmdlet or one native binary per SSH body (no compound PowerShell bodies)

content.content:
100	`'...'` as a string literal. Use single quotes for PS literals inside the
101	outer `"..."` — they nest reliably through every layer.
102	
103	```bash
104	# ✅ Single-quoted PS literals inside outer "..."
105	ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'username'\""
106	```
107	
108	### ✅ WSp-104: WSp-102's lazy-flush behavior is transport-agnostic
109	
110	Lazy-flush is a property of the body, not of how PowerShell was launched —
111	the same `Get-LocalUser; exit 0` body emits empty stdout through every
112	transport. The fix (Out-String materialization, or WSp-101's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0`)
113	applies uniformly.
114	
115	### ❌ WSp-105: PowerShell bodies are single expressions
116	
117	A PowerShell body (`powershell -Command "<body>"` or capture variant)
118	MUST be exactly one of:
119	
120	1. One cmdlet call (arguments, parameters, pipelined formatters like
121	   `| Out-String` or `| Out-Null`).
122	2. One native binary call.
123	3. One native binary with an inner-shell body
124	   (e.g. `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`).
125	
126	The enumeration is constructive. Forbidden at body top level: `if`,
127	`try`/`catch`, `foreach`, `while`, `switch`, `do`, `&&`/`||`,
128	`;`-separated statements, intermediate `$var` assignments, ternary `?:`.
129	A single `if`-guarded effect like
130	`if (Test-Path '...') { Remove-Item '...' }` is also forbidden — the
131	body decides AND acts; decisions belong in bash. See CDD below.
132	
133	The wrapper-side prelude
134	(`$ErrorActionPreference='Stop'; $env:WSL_UTF8=1; $LASTEXITCODE=0; <body>; if ...`)
135	is library code; the rule constrains caller bodies only. The wrapper's
136	exact three-slot shape (prelude / body / postlude) is the minimum-viable
137	scaffolding given WSp-101, WSp-102, WSp-105, and WSp-109 acting in
138	combination — see
139	`Memos/memo-20260511-windows-transport-wrapper-postlude-synthesis.md`
140	for the integrative rationale and the elimination argument showing why
141	each token is load-bearing.
142	
143	```bash
144	# ❌ Compound state machine in PS body
145	priv_ps "\$sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_user}').SID.Value; \$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null; New-ItemProperty \$path -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
146	
147	# ❌ Single `if`-guard — body decides AND acts; state machine belongs in bash
148	priv_ps "if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }"
149	
150	# ✅ Bash-orchestrated single-expression calls
151	z_sid=$(ps_capture "(Get-LocalUser '${z_user}').SID.Value") || die "..."
152	z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
153	priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
154	priv_ps "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
155	
156	# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
157	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || die "..."
158	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
159	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || die "..."
160	fi
161	```
162	
163	### ❌ WSp-106: Don't interpolate strings via PowerShell when bash can build them
164	
165	The bash caller builds the string by expanding `${bash_var}` before
166	sending; the PS body receives a literal. Bash escape rules cascade once;
167	adding PS-side interpolation (`"...$var..."`) layers another substitution
168	model on top and compounds with WSp-107's native-binary quoting traps.
169	
170	```bash
171	# ❌ PS-side string concatenation
172	priv_ps "\$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null"
173	
174	# ✅ Bash builds the string; PS receives a literal
175	z_path="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
176	priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_path}' -Force | Out-Null"
177	```
178	
179	### ❌ WSp-107: Don't interpolate variables through PowerShell to native binaries
180	
181	PowerShell's argv handling for native Windows binaries (reg.exe, sc.exe,
182	schtasks.exe) mishandles embedded spaces — a path containing `Windows NT`
183	breaks `reg.exe` invoked through PS even via a PS variable. Use PS cmdlets
184	for PS-native effects (registry as PS-drive, ACLs, account ops); for
185	native-binary effects, pass already-resolved bash literals or invoke
186	through cmd.exe directly.
187	
188	```bash
189	# ❌ Native binary with PS interpolation — argv quoting may eat path spaces
190	priv_ps "reg.exe add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\...\\\$sid\" /v X /t REG_SZ /d Y /f"
191	
192	# ✅ PS cmdlet handles PS-drive registry paths cleanly
193	priv_ps "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
194	```
195	
196	### ❌ WSp-108: Error suppression is not idempotency
197	
198	Error-suppression flags on destructive cmdlets (`-ErrorAction Ignore`,
199	`-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`, `2>$null`, `try { ... } catch { }`)
200	swallow **every** error class — permission denied, file locked, ACL
201	refusal, path-too-long — converting failure into apparent success.
202	Forbidden on destructive actions. Idempotency MUST come from explicit
203	state capture, then unconditional action (see CDD).
204	
205	Distinguish suppressing **output** from suppressing **errors**:
206	`| Out-Null` discards the return object (cmdlet still throws); `-ErrorAction
207	Ignore` discards the error (cmdlet returns success on real failure).
208	WSp-108 forbids the latter on destructive actions.
209	
210	Probe-side use is permitted on cmdlets whose purpose is to *return state*
211	(`Get-LocalUser -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` where "absent" is the
212	answer); forbidden on cmdlets whose purpose is to *change state*.
213	
214	```bash
215	# ❌ Error-suppression masquerading as idempotency
216	priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' -ErrorAction Ignore"
217	priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
218	priv_ps "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist} 2>\$null; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0"
219	
220	# ✅ CDD: capture, decide, dispatch unconditionally
221	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || die "..."
222	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
223	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || die "..."
224	fi
225	
226	# ✅ Probe-side suppression — Get-LocalUser is non-destructive, "user not found" is the answer
227	priv_ps "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
228	```
229	
230	**Exception — nuclear-cleanup absent-state collapse.** When a step's
231	semantic intent is "state X is absent" rather than "transition X from
232	present to absent," dispatch-side `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` on the
233	destructive cmdlet is permitted under **all** of:
234	
235	a. **Narrow failure spectrum at the callsite.** The realistic non-absent
236	   failure modes are catastrophic environmental conditions (admin
237	   permission revoked, OS / kernel corruption, transport collapse) that
238	   would also break adjacent operations in the same SSH session — not
239	   file-locking, ACL friction, path-encoding, or concurrent-writer races.
240	   The callsite's context must justify this narrowness explicitly (e.g.,
241	   admin-context SAM operations on a fixed user name; not arbitrary
242	   filesystem paths).
243	
244	b. **Downstream verification.** A later step in the same orchestration
245	   asserts an end-state that would fail if the collapsed step silently
246	   failed — typically a provision step that errors on "already exists,"
247	   or an explicit invigilate-style absence probe. A head NOTE describing
248	   the phase as best-effort does NOT satisfy this leg; there must be a
249	   concrete downstream operation whose failure surfaces silent partial
250	   cleanup.
251	
252	c. **Single suppression point, named form.** Exactly one
253	   `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` on the destructive cmdlet itself — not
254	   `-ErrorAction Ignore` (which discards the error from `$Error`,
255	   destroying the forensic trail), not `2>$null`, not
256	   `try { ... } catch { }`, not stacked with any of the above.
257	   `SilentlyContinue` retains the error in `$Error` for post-mortem
258	   inspection; the other forms forfeit that.
259	
260	When ANY of (a)-(c) fail, probe-then-act per the main rule remains
261	required.
262	
263	```bash
264	# ✅ Exception qualifies — Remove-LocalUser in nuclear-cleanup obliterate phase
265	# (a) narrow spectrum: admin context, SAM database, fixed workload user name;
266	#     realistic failures are "absent" or SAM-level catastrophic.
267	# (b) downstream catch: subsequent New-LocalUser fails on "account already exists".
268	# (c) single -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue on the destructive cmdlet.
269	priv_ps "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
270	
271	# ❌ Exception does NOT qualify — arbitrary filesystem path
272	# (a) fails: Remove-Item on Cygwin home / user-chosen path has rich failure
273	#     classes (NTFS ACL friction, junction loops, file-locked-by-process).
274	# Stay on probe-then-act per the main rule.
275	priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
276	```
277	
278	The probe-side carve-out (`Get-LocalUser -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`,
279	returning state) and this dispatch-side exception are complementary, not
280	interchangeable. Probe-side suppression is always safe because the cmdlet
281	is non-destructive. Dispatch-side suppression requires all three
282	conditions because the cmdlet changes state and the suppression hides
283	non-absent failures by construction.
284	
285	### ✅ WSp-109: Wrapper helpers escape body `"` unconditionally
286	
287	Any bash helper that wraps caller-provided `${z_body}` inside outer
288	`"..."` argv quoting MUST apply `${z_body//\"/\\\"}` before
289	interpolation. The transform is a helper-author invariant, not a
290	body-author burden — body authors stay free to write inner `"`
291	literally (e.g., `Get-CimInstance -Filter "LocalPath='...'"`), and the
292	wrapper guarantees passthrough across the cmd.exe argv layer.
293	
294	WSt-102 describes the mechanism (cmd.exe's tokenizer honors `\"`
295	inside `"..."`); WSp-109 names the helper-author obligation. Omitting
296	the transform produces malformed argv on the wire: cmd.exe terminates
297	the outer quoted string at the first inner `"`, and the remote
298	interpreter sees fragmented positional arguments. Failure signature:
299

`'...'` as a string literal. Use single quotes for PS literals inside the
outer `"..."` — they nest reliably through every layer.

```bash
# ✅ Single-quoted PS literals inside outer "..."
ssh ... "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-LocalUser -Name 'username'\""
```

### ✅ WSp-104: WSp-102's lazy-flush behavior is transport-agnostic

Lazy-flush is a property of the body, not of how PowerShell was launched —
the same `Get-LocalUser; exit 0` body emits empty stdout through every
transport. The fix (Out-String materialization, or WSp-101's `$LASTEXITCODE = 0`)
applies uniformly.

### ❌ WSp-105: PowerShell bodies are single expressions

A PowerShell body (`powershell -Command "<body>"` or capture variant)
MUST be exactly one of:

1. One cmdlet call (arguments, parameters, pipelined formatters like
   `| Out-String` or `| Out-Null`).
2. One native binary call.
3. One native binary with an inner-shell body
   (e.g. `wsl.exe ... bash -c "..."`).

The enumeration is constructive. Forbidden at body top level: `if`,
`try`/`catch`, `foreach`, `while`, `switch`, `do`, `&&`/`||`,
`;`-separated statements, intermediate `$var` assignments, ternary `?:`.
A single `if`-guarded effect like
`if (Test-Path '...') { Remove-Item '...' }` is also forbidden — the
body decides AND acts; decisions belong in bash. See CDD below.

The wrapper-side prelude
(`$ErrorActionPreference='Stop'; $env:WSL_UTF8=1; $LASTEXITCODE=0; <body>; if ...`)
is library code; the rule constrains caller bodies only. The wrapper's
exact three-slot shape (prelude / body / postlude) is the minimum-viable
scaffolding given WSp-101, WSp-102, WSp-105, and WSp-109 acting in
combination — see
`Memos/memo-20260511-windows-transport-wrapper-postlude-synthesis.md`
for the integrative rationale and the elimination argument showing why
each token is load-bearing.

```bash
# ❌ Compound state machine in PS body
priv_ps "\$sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_user}').SID.Value; \$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null; New-ItemProperty \$path -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"

# ❌ Single `if`-guard — body decides AND acts; state machine belongs in bash
priv_ps "if (Test-Path '${z_tar_path}') { Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' }"

# ✅ Bash-orchestrated single-expression calls
z_sid=$(ps_capture "(Get-LocalUser '${z_user}').SID.Value") || die "..."
z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
priv_ps "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"

# ✅ Single guarded effect repaired via Capture-Decide-Dispatch
z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || die "..."
if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || die "..."
fi
```

### ❌ WSp-106: Don't interpolate strings via PowerShell when bash can build them

The bash caller builds the string by expanding `${bash_var}` before
sending; the PS body receives a literal. Bash escape rules cascade once;
adding PS-side interpolation (`"...$var..."`) layers another substitution
model on top and compounds with WSp-107's native-binary quoting traps.

```bash
# ❌ PS-side string concatenation
priv_ps "\$path='HKLM:\\...\\' + \$sid; New-Item \$path -Force | Out-Null"

# ✅ Bash builds the string; PS receives a literal
z_path="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_path}' -Force | Out-Null"
```

### ❌ WSp-107: Don't interpolate variables through PowerShell to native binaries

PowerShell's argv handling for native Windows binaries (reg.exe, sc.exe,
schtasks.exe) mishandles embedded spaces — a path containing `Windows NT`
breaks `reg.exe` invoked through PS even via a PS variable. Use PS cmdlets
for PS-native effects (registry as PS-drive, ACLs, account ops); for
native-binary effects, pass already-resolved bash literals or invoke
through cmd.exe directly.

```bash
# ❌ Native binary with PS interpolation — argv quoting may eat path spaces
priv_ps "reg.exe add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\...\\\$sid\" /v X /t REG_SZ /d Y /f"

# ✅ PS cmdlet handles PS-drive registry paths cleanly
priv_ps "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'X' -Value 'Y' -Force | Out-Null"
```

### ❌ WSp-108: Error suppression is not idempotency

Error-suppression flags on destructive cmdlets (`-ErrorAction Ignore`,
`-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`, `2>$null`, `try { ... } catch { }`)
swallow **every** error class — permission denied, file locked, ACL
refusal, path-too-long — converting failure into apparent success.
Forbidden on destructive actions. Idempotency MUST come from explicit
state capture, then unconditional action (see CDD).

Distinguish suppressing **output** from suppressing **errors**:
`| Out-Null` discards the return object (cmdlet still throws); `-ErrorAction
Ignore` discards the error (cmdlet returns success on real failure).
WSp-108 forbids the latter on destructive actions.

Probe-side use is permitted on cmdlets whose purpose is to *return state*
(`Get-LocalUser -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` where "absent" is the
answer); forbidden on cmdlets whose purpose is to *change state*.

```bash
# ❌ Error-suppression masquerading as idempotency
priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}' -ErrorAction Ignore"
priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
priv_ps "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist} 2>\$null; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0"

# ✅ CDD: capture, decide, dispatch unconditionally
z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_tar_path}'") || die "..."
if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_tar_path}'" || die "..."
fi

# ✅ Probe-side suppression — Get-LocalUser is non-destructive, "user not found" is the answer
priv_ps "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
```

**Exception — nuclear-cleanup absent-state collapse.** When a step's
semantic intent is "state X is absent" rather than "transition X from
present to absent," dispatch-side `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` on the
destructive cmdlet is permitted under **all** of:

a. **Narrow failure spectrum at the callsite.** The realistic non-absent
   failure modes are catastrophic environmental conditions (admin
   permission revoked, OS / kernel corruption, transport collapse) that
   would also break adjacent operations in the same SSH session — not
   file-locking, ACL friction, path-encoding, or concurrent-writer races.
   The callsite's context must justify this narrowness explicitly (e.g.,
   admin-context SAM operations on a fixed user name; not arbitrary
   filesystem paths).

b. **Downstream verification.** A later step in the same orchestration
   asserts an end-state that would fail if the collapsed step silently
   failed — typically a provision step that errors on "already exists,"
   or an explicit invigilate-style absence probe. A head NOTE describing
   the phase as best-effort does NOT satisfy this leg; there must be a
   concrete downstream operation whose failure surfaces silent partial
   cleanup.

c. **Single suppression point, named form.** Exactly one
   `-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` on the destructive cmdlet itself — not
   `-ErrorAction Ignore` (which discards the error from `$Error`,
   destroying the forensic trail), not `2>$null`, not
   `try { ... } catch { }`, not stacked with any of the above.
   `SilentlyContinue` retains the error in `$Error` for post-mortem
   inspection; the other forms forfeit that.

When ANY of (a)-(c) fail, probe-then-act per the main rule remains
required.

```bash
# ✅ Exception qualifies — Remove-LocalUser in nuclear-cleanup obliterate phase
# (a) narrow spectrum: admin context, SAM database, fixed workload user name;
#     realistic failures are "absent" or SAM-level catastrophic.
# (b) downstream catch: subsequent New-LocalUser fails on "account already exists".
# (c) single -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue on the destructive cmdlet.
priv_ps "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"

# ❌ Exception does NOT qualify — arbitrary filesystem path
# (a) fails: Remove-Item on Cygwin home / user-chosen path has rich failure
#     classes (NTFS ACL friction, junction loops, file-locked-by-process).
# Stay on probe-then-act per the main rule.
priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue"
```

The probe-side carve-out (`Get-LocalUser -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`,
returning state) and this dispatch-side exception are complementary, not
interchangeable. Probe-side suppression is always safe because the cmdlet
is non-destructive. Dispatch-side suppression requires all three
conditions because the cmdlet changes state and the suppression hides
non-absent failures by construction.

### ✅ WSp-109: Wrapper helpers escape body `"` unconditionally

Any bash helper that wraps caller-provided `${z_body}` inside outer
`"..."` argv quoting MUST apply `${z_body//\"/\\\"}` before
interpolation. The transform is a helper-author invariant, not a
body-author burden — body authors stay free to write inner `"`
literally (e.g., `Get-CimInstance -Filter "LocalPath='...'"`), and the
wrapper guarantees passthrough across the cmd.exe argv layer.

WSt-102 describes the mechanism (cmd.exe's tokenizer honors `\"`
inside `"..."`); WSp-109 names the helper-author obligation. Omitting
the transform produces malformed argv on the wire: cmd.exe terminates
the outer quoted string at the first inner `"`, and the remote
interpreter sees fragmented positional arguments. Failure signature:

lastPrompt: Mount ₢A-ABH (caparison-windows-decompose) under ₣A-. Docket carries   four locked decisions from the prior chat — read via gazette after   jjx_orient.    Field state on bujn-winpc: sshd_config revert…

content.content:
450	cmd.exe layer is not what processes shell variable expansion in transit
451	(see WSt-104 for what does, on the w-letter path).
452	
453	### ❌ WSt-104: wsl.exe substitutes `$name` and `${name}` in argv
454	
455	Wsl.exe's argv-to-Linux processor performs `$name`/`${name}` substitution
456	against its Linux-side root-shell environment before bash receives the
457	body; undefined names substitute to empty. Cygwin and cmd.exe alone do
458	not. `$(...)` is unaffected (the `(` after `$` does not match the token
459	shape).
460	
461	Per-letter escape table:
462	
463	| Letter | Path                                       | `$name`     | `${name}`     | `$(...)` |
464	|--------|--------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------|----------|
465	| b      | direct ssh to Linux/Mac                    | none        | none          | none     |
466	| c      | cmd.exe → cygwin bash                      | none        | none          | none     |
467	| w      | cmd.exe → wsl.exe → bash                   | `\$name`    | `\${name}`    | none     |
468	
469	```bash
470	# ❌ Fails on w-letter — wsl.exe substitutes $ztmp to empty before bash runs
471	ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo $ztmp"'
472	# stdout: (empty)
473	
474	# ✅ \$ defers expansion to bash
475	ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo \$ztmp"'
476	# stdout: HELLO
477	```
478	
479	### ✅ WSt-105: Exit-code propagation is reliable across transports
480	
481	Non-zero exit codes propagate cleanly through cmd.exe direct, wsl.exe →
482	bash, cygwin bash, PowerShell, and Windows native binaries via wsl.exe
483	interop. WSs-104's single statement IS the body's exit code; the caller
484	absorbs via `|| die "..."`. No `set -e` needed inside the body (nothing
485	after one statement). `set -o pipefail` IS needed for shape-2 pipelines
486	when first-failure surfacing is the intent.
487	
488	```bash
489	# Probe: false propagation through w-letter
490	ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "false"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
491	# EXIT=1
492	```
493	
494	### ❌ WSt-106: Single quotes are LITERAL characters in cmd.exe-direct transport
495	
496	When routed `bash (curia) → ssh → cmd.exe → <Windows-native binary>` with
497	no PowerShell or remote-bash layer, single quotes pass through to the
498	binary as argv bytes. Pick the quote form by the **innermost interpreter**:
499	
500	```bash
501	# ❌ wsl.exe sees paths with literal `'...'` quotes and fails.
502	ssh "${USER}@${HOST}" "wsl.exe --import dist 'C:\Users\u\dist-fs' '...' --version 2"
503	
504	# ✅ \"...\" reaches the wire as "..."; cmd.exe strips, wsl.exe sees clean paths.
505	ssh "${USER}@${HOST}" "wsl.exe --import dist \"C:\Users\u\dist-fs\" \"...\" --version 2"
506	```
507	
508	| Innermost interpreter         | Quote form for embedded args                                       |
509	|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
510	| PowerShell                    | `'...'` (PS string literal)                                        |
511	| Remote bash (`bash -c "..."`) | inner `'...'` (bash strips)                                        |
512	| cmd.exe → native binary       | `\"...\"` (becomes literal `"..."` on the wire; cmd.exe strips)    |
513	
514	## Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern
515	
516	Bash owns the state machine; the remote-side body is one effect. Any
517	decision that would otherwise live in a remote body decomposes into:
518	
519	1. **Capture** — `ps_capture "<single-expression probe>"` (WSp-105-clean)
520	   or `$(priv_bash LETTER "<single-statement probe>")` (WSs-104-clean)
521	   returns stdout into a bash variable. Absorb with `|| die "..."` if the
522	   probe must succeed, or `|| z_var=""` / `|| z_exit=$?` if an absent
523	   fundus state is legitimate pre-condition.
524	
525	2. **Decide** — bash conditional. PS booleans: `[[ "$x" == "True" ]]`
526	   (see boolean serialization below). Text/list: `grep -qFx PATTERN <<<"$x"`,
527	   `[[ "$x" == ... ]]`, or `case`. Bash exit-status probes:
528	   `[[ ${z_exit} -eq 0 ]]`.
529	
530	3. **Dispatch** — `priv_ps "<effect>"` or `priv_bash LETTER "<effect>"`
531	   runs the unconditional action on branches that need it.
532	
533	### PowerShell boolean serialization
534	
535	`Test-Path`, `Test-Connection`, and other PS-bool-emitting cmdlets
536	serialize as literal strings `"True"` / `"False"` through `ps_capture`.
537	Use `[[ "${z_var}" == "True" ]]`, not `[[ -n "${z_var}" ]]` — `False` is
538	also non-empty.
539	
540	### Capture failure absorption
541	
542	`ps_capture` returns non-zero on ssh transport failure, PS body throwing
543	under `$ErrorActionPreference=Stop`, native-binary non-zero exit, or
544	wrapper-trailer fire. Two absorption shapes:
545	
546	```bash
547	# Probe must succeed — die loudly on transport/body failure
548	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
549	  || die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
550	
551	# Empty result on probe failure is a valid pre-state — absorb silently
552	z_wsl_list=""
553	z_wsl_list=$(ps_capture "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
554	  || z_wsl_list=""
555	```
556	
557	Use the loud form by default. Use the silent form only when the absent
558	state is a legitimate pre-condition.
559	
560	## Idempotency Exemplars
561	
562	Ready-made CDD shapes; copy and adjust variable names.
563	
564	### File presence → conditional remove
565	
566	```bash
567	local z_present
568	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
569	  || die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
570	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
571	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_path}'" \
572	    || die "Failed to remove ${z_path}"
573	fi
574	```
575	
576	### Directory presence → conditional remove (recursive)
577	
578	```bash
579	local z_present
580	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_dir}'") \
581	  || die "Failed to probe ${z_dir}"
582	if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
583	  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}'" \
584	    || die "Failed to remove ${z_dir}"
585	fi
586	```
587	
588	### WSL distro membership → conditional unregister
589	
590	Fresh-WSL host with no installed distros is a legitimate pre-state, so
591	the list capture absorbs failure silently.
592	
593	```bash
594	local z_wsl_list=""
595	z_wsl_list=$(ps_capture "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
596	  || z_wsl_list=""
597	if grep -qFx "${z_dist}" <<<"${z_wsl_list}"; then
598	  priv_ps "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist}" \
599	    || die "Failed to unregister ${z_dist}"
600	fi
601	```
602	
603	### Local user existence → conditional removal
604	
605	`-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` permitted per WSp-108's probe-side carve-out.
606	
607	```bash
608	local z_user_state
609	z_user_state=$(ps_capture \
610	  "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue") \
611	  || die "Failed to probe local user ${z_user}"
612	z_user_state="${z_user_state//[$'\r\n\t ']/}"
613	if [[ -n "${z_user_state}" ]]; then
614	  priv_ps "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}'" \
615	    || die "Failed to remove local user ${z_user}"
616	fi
617	```
618	
619	### Service running state → conditional start
620	
621	```bash
622	local z_service_state
623	z_service_state=$(ps_capture \
624	  "(Get-Service -Name '${z_svc}').Status") \
625	  || die "Failed to probe service ${z_svc}"
626	if [[ "${z_service_state}" == "Stopped" ]]; then
627	  priv_ps "Start-Service -Name '${z_svc}'" \
628	    || die "Failed to start service ${z_svc}"
629	fi
630	```
631	
632	### Registry key absence → conditional creation
633	
634	```bash
635	local z_present
636	z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_regkey}'") \
637	  || die "Failed to probe registry key ${z_regkey}"
638	if [[ "${z_present}" != "True" ]]; then
639	  priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null" \
640	    || die "Failed to create registry key ${z_regkey}"
641	fi
642	```
643	
644	## Wrapper Discipline
645	
646	All three wrappers compose outer `"..."` around `${z_body}` and so apply
647	the WSp-109 escape transform unconditionally.
648	
649	### PowerShell wrapper
650	
651	```bash
652	local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
653	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
654	    "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0; ${z_body_escaped}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
655	```
656	
657	Wrapper prelude:
658	- `\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` — PS errors terminate
659	- `\$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1` — wsl.exe (when called from PS body) emits UTF-8
660	- `\$LASTEXITCODE = 0` — initialize to defeat the null trap (WSp-101)
661	- Trailing `if ... exit ...` — propagate native command exit codes
662	
663	Body authoring follows WSp-102 through WSp-108.
664	
665	### Bash-via-wsl.exe wrapper (w-letter)
666	
667	```bash
668	local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
669	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
670	    "wsl.exe --distribution ${DIST} --user root bash -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
671	```
672	
673	Body authoring follows the WSs- and WSt- rule families, with WSt-104's
674	w-letter escape table: `$name` → `\$name`; `${name}` → `\${name}`;
675	`$(...)` unescaped.
676	
677	### Bash-via-cygwin wrapper (c-letter)
678	
679	```bash
680	local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
681	ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
682	    "C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
683	```
684	
685	Body authoring follows the WSs- and WSt- rule families. Per WSt-104's
686	c-letter row, `$name`, `${name}`, and `$(...)` all pass unescaped —
687	cmd.exe alone does no shell substitution.
688	
689	## Deferred
690	
691	The b-letter path (direct ssh to Linux/Mac) bypasses every Windows layer;
692	standard BCG body discipline applies. When adding a non-Windows target,
693	run analog probes substituting plain bash for wsl.exe / cygwin.
694	
695	## Verification Probe Template
696	
697	```
698	# Probe N: <claim>
699	# Pre-state: <relevant remote state>
700	
701	<priv-ssh wrapper> <node> "<failing form>"
702	# stdout: <observed>; exit: <observed>
703	
704	<priv-ssh wrapper> <node> "<repaired form>"
705	# stdout: <observed>; exit: <observed>
706	```
707	
708	Probes are read-only where possible; mutating probes must declare cleanup.
709	
710	## Acronym Registry
711	
712	| Term | Expansion |
713	|------|-----------|
714	| WSG | Windows Scripting Guide (this document) |
715	| OpenSSH-Win32 | The Win32 port of OpenSSH bundled with Windows 10+ |
716	| Curia | The local invoking machine |
717	| Fundus | The remote target machine |
718	
719	## Related Documents

cmd.exe layer is not what processes shell variable expansion in transit
(see WSt-104 for what does, on the w-letter path).

### ❌ WSt-104: wsl.exe substitutes `$name` and `${name}` in argv

Wsl.exe's argv-to-Linux processor performs `$name`/`${name}` substitution
against its Linux-side root-shell environment before bash receives the
body; undefined names substitute to empty. Cygwin and cmd.exe alone do
not. `$(...)` is unaffected (the `(` after `$` does not match the token
shape).

Per-letter escape table:

| Letter | Path                                       | `$name`     | `${name}`     | `$(...)` |
|--------|--------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------|----------|
| b      | direct ssh to Linux/Mac                    | none        | none          | none     |
| c      | cmd.exe → cygwin bash                      | none        | none          | none     |
| w      | cmd.exe → wsl.exe → bash                   | `\$name`    | `\${name}`    | none     |

```bash
# ❌ Fails on w-letter — wsl.exe substitutes $ztmp to empty before bash runs
ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo $ztmp"'
# stdout: (empty)

# ✅ \$ defers expansion to bash
ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "ztmp=HELLO; echo \$ztmp"'
# stdout: HELLO
```

### ✅ WSt-105: Exit-code propagation is reliable across transports

Non-zero exit codes propagate cleanly through cmd.exe direct, wsl.exe →
bash, cygwin bash, PowerShell, and Windows native binaries via wsl.exe
interop. WSs-104's single statement IS the body's exit code; the caller
absorbs via `|| die "..."`. No `set -e` needed inside the body (nothing
after one statement). `set -o pipefail` IS needed for shape-2 pipelines
when first-failure surfacing is the intent.

```bash
# Probe: false propagation through w-letter
ssh ... 'wsl.exe --distribution <dist> --user root bash -c "false"'; echo "EXIT=$?"
# EXIT=1
```

### ❌ WSt-106: Single quotes are LITERAL characters in cmd.exe-direct transport

When routed `bash (curia) → ssh → cmd.exe → <Windows-native binary>` with
no PowerShell or remote-bash layer, single quotes pass through to the
binary as argv bytes. Pick the quote form by the **innermost interpreter**:

```bash
# ❌ wsl.exe sees paths with literal `'...'` quotes and fails.
ssh "${USER}@${HOST}" "wsl.exe --import dist 'C:\Users\u\dist-fs' '...' --version 2"

# ✅ \"...\" reaches the wire as "..."; cmd.exe strips, wsl.exe sees clean paths.
ssh "${USER}@${HOST}" "wsl.exe --import dist \"C:\Users\u\dist-fs\" \"...\" --version 2"
```

| Innermost interpreter         | Quote form for embedded args                                       |
|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| PowerShell                    | `'...'` (PS string literal)                                        |
| Remote bash (`bash -c "..."`) | inner `'...'` (bash strips)                                        |
| cmd.exe → native binary       | `\"...\"` (becomes literal `"..."` on the wire; cmd.exe strips)    |

## Capture-Decide-Dispatch Pattern

Bash owns the state machine; the remote-side body is one effect. Any
decision that would otherwise live in a remote body decomposes into:

1. **Capture** — `ps_capture "<single-expression probe>"` (WSp-105-clean)
   or `$(priv_bash LETTER "<single-statement probe>")` (WSs-104-clean)
   returns stdout into a bash variable. Absorb with `|| die "..."` if the
   probe must succeed, or `|| z_var=""` / `|| z_exit=$?` if an absent
   fundus state is legitimate pre-condition.

2. **Decide** — bash conditional. PS booleans: `[[ "$x" == "True" ]]`
   (see boolean serialization below). Text/list: `grep -qFx PATTERN <<<"$x"`,
   `[[ "$x" == ... ]]`, or `case`. Bash exit-status probes:
   `[[ ${z_exit} -eq 0 ]]`.

3. **Dispatch** — `priv_ps "<effect>"` or `priv_bash LETTER "<effect>"`
   runs the unconditional action on branches that need it.

### PowerShell boolean serialization

`Test-Path`, `Test-Connection`, and other PS-bool-emitting cmdlets
serialize as literal strings `"True"` / `"False"` through `ps_capture`.
Use `[[ "${z_var}" == "True" ]]`, not `[[ -n "${z_var}" ]]` — `False` is
also non-empty.

### Capture failure absorption

`ps_capture` returns non-zero on ssh transport failure, PS body throwing
under `$ErrorActionPreference=Stop`, native-binary non-zero exit, or
wrapper-trailer fire. Two absorption shapes:

```bash
# Probe must succeed — die loudly on transport/body failure
z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
  || die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"

# Empty result on probe failure is a valid pre-state — absorb silently
z_wsl_list=""
z_wsl_list=$(ps_capture "wsl.exe --list --quiet") \
  || z_wsl_list=""
```

Use the loud form by default. Use the silent form only when the absent
state is a legitimate pre-condition.

## Idempotency Exemplars

Ready-made CDD shapes; copy and adjust variable names.

### File presence → conditional remove

```bash
local z_present
z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_path}'") \
  || die "Failed to probe ${z_path}"
if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Force '${z_path}'" \
    || die "Failed to remove ${z_path}"
fi
```

### Directory presence → conditional remove (recursive)

```bash
local z_present
z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_dir}'") \
  || die "Failed to probe ${z_dir}"
if [[ "${z_present}" == "True" ]]; then
  priv_ps "Remove-Item -Recurse -Force '${z_dir}'" \
    || die "Failed to remove ${z_dir}"
fi
```

### WSL distro membership → conditional unregister

Fresh-WSL host with no installed distros is a legitimate pre-state, so
the list capture absorbs failure silently.

```bash
local z_wsl_list=""

  || z_wsl_list=""

  priv_ps "wsl.exe --unregister ${z_dist}" \
    || die "Failed to unregister ${z_dist}"

```

### Local user existence → conditional removal

`-ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` permitted per WSp-108's probe-side carve-out.

```bash
local z_user_state
z_user_state=$(ps_capture \
  "Get-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue") \
  || die "Failed to probe local user ${z_user}"
z_user_state="${z_user_state//[$'\r\n\t ']/}"
if [[ -n "${z_user_state}" ]]; then
  priv_ps "Remove-LocalUser -Name '${z_user}'" \
    || die "Failed to remove local user ${z_user}"
fi
```

### Service running state → conditional start

```bash
local z_service_state
z_service_state=$(ps_capture \
  "(Get-Service -Name '${z_svc}').Status") \
  || die "Failed to probe service ${z_svc}"
if [[ "${z_service_state}" == "Stopped" ]]; then
  priv_ps "Start-Service -Name '${z_svc}'" \
    || die "Failed to start service ${z_svc}"
fi
```

### Registry key absence → conditional creation

```bash
local z_present
z_present=$(ps_capture "Test-Path '${z_regkey}'") \
  || die "Failed to probe registry key ${z_regkey}"
if [[ "${z_present}" != "True" ]]; then
  priv_ps "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null" \
    || die "Failed to create registry key ${z_regkey}"

```

## Wrapper Discipline

All three wrappers compose outer `"..."` around `${z_body}` and so apply
the WSp-109 escape transform unconditionally.

### PowerShell wrapper

```bash
local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
    "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0; ${z_body_escaped}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
```

Wrapper prelude:
- `\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` — PS errors terminate
- `\$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1` — wsl.exe (when called from PS body) emits UTF-8
- `\$LASTEXITCODE = 0` — initialize to defeat the null trap (WSp-101)
- Trailing `if ... exit ...` — propagate native command exit codes

Body authoring follows WSp-102 through WSp-108.

### Bash-via-wsl.exe wrapper (w-letter)

```bash
local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
    "wsl.exe --distribution ${DIST} --user root bash -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
```

Body authoring follows the WSs- and WSt- rule families, with WSt-104's
w-letter escape table: `$name` → `\$name`; `${name}` → `\${name}`;
`$(...)` unescaped.

### Bash-via-cygwin wrapper (c-letter)

```bash
local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
ssh ... "${USER}@${HOST}" \
    "C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
```

Body authoring follows the WSs- and WSt- rule families. Per WSt-104's
c-letter row, `$name`, `${name}`, and `$(...)` all pass unescaped —
cmd.exe alone does no shell substitution.

## Deferred

The b-letter path (direct ssh to Linux/Mac) bypasses every Windows layer;
standard BCG body discipline applies. When adding a non-Windows target,
run analog probes substituting plain bash for wsl.exe / cygwin.

## Verification Probe Template

```
# Probe N: <claim>
# Pre-state: <relevant remote state>

<priv-ssh wrapper> <node> "<failing form>"
# stdout: <observed>; exit: <observed>

<priv-ssh wrapper> <node> "<repaired form>"
# stdout: <observed>; exit: <observed>
```

Probes are read-only where possible; mutating probes must declare cleanup.

## Acronym Registry

| Term | Expansion |
|------|-----------|
| WSG | Windows Scripting Guide (this document) |
| OpenSSH-Win32 | The Win32 port of OpenSSH bundled with Windows 10+ |
| Curia | The local invoking machine |
| Fundus | The remote target machine |

## Related Documents

552	      "${z_remote_invoker} -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
553	}
554	
555	# zbujb_admin_powershell BODY... -- run a PowerShell statement chain on
556	# the remote node as the privileged user. Prepends discipline prelude:
557	# $ErrorActionPreference='Stop' (PS errors terminate), $env:WSL_UTF8=1
558	# (wsl.exe emits UTF-8), $LASTEXITCODE=0 (initialize so the trailing
559	# if-check doesn't trip on the $null default — $null -ne 0 evaluates True
560	# in PowerShell typed comparison, which would fire `exit $null` and
561	# discard buffered object-formatter output for cmdlets like Get-LocalUser
562	# whose tables haven't flushed to stdout yet). Trailing if-check
563	# propagates $LASTEXITCODE on non-zero from native commands in the body.
564	# Caller redirects stdout/stderr; returns ssh's exit code.
565	zbujb_admin_powershell() {
566	  zbujb_sentinel
567	  test $# -ge 1 \
568	    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_powershell: PowerShell command body required"
569	  local -r z_body="$*"
570	  local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
571	
572	  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"        \
573	      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"             \
574	      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"      \
575	      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}"  \
576	      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"  \
577	      "${BUJB_ps_invoke_command} \"${BUJB_ps_prelude} ${z_body_escaped}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
578	}
579	
580	# zbujb_powershell_capture ROLE BODY -- run a single PS expression on the
581	# remote node as the named role and emit its stdout. Windows CR is stripped
582	# via bash parameter expansion (no subprocess) so $(...) yields a clean
583	# value. Single-expression discipline per WSG-PS-5; multi-statement bodies
584	# do not belong here. Caller || buc_die on capture failure.
585	# ROLE: zbujb_privileged | zbujb_workload
586	zbujb_powershell_capture() {
587	  zbujb_sentinel
588	  test $# -ge 2 \
589	    || buc_die "zbujb_powershell_capture: ROLE BODY required"
590	  local -r z_role="$1"; shift
591	  local -r z_body="$*"
592	  local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109
593	
594	  local z_key z_user
595	  case "${z_role}" in
596	    zbujb_privileged) z_key="${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"; z_user="${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}" ;;
597	    zbujb_workload)   z_key="${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}";   z_user="${BUJB_workload_user}"   ;;
598	    *)                buc_die "zbujb_powershell_capture: unknown role '${z_role}' (zbujb_privileged|zbujb_workload)" ;;
599	  esac
600	
601	  local z_out z_exit=0
602	  z_out=$(ssh -i "${z_key}"                       \
603	              "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"         \
604	              -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"  \
605	              -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}" \
606	              "${z_user}@${BURN_HOST}"            \
607	              "${BUJB_ps_invoke_command} \"${BUJB_ps_prelude} ${z_body_escaped}\"") \
608	    || z_exit=$?
609	
610	  printf '%s' "${z_out//$'\r'/}"
611	  return "${z_exit}"
612	}
613	
614	# zbujb_workload_ssh REMOTE_CMD -- execute a Windows-native command line
615	# as the workload user via SSH. The default Windows OpenSSH shell is
616	# cmd.exe; the command line goes through cmd.exe's argv layer to the named
617	# binary. Used by garrison-w for `wsl.exe --import` (which writes to the
618	# workload's HKCU\Lxss because the SSH session is a real workload logon)
619	# and `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "..."`
620	# invocations that follow the import. Caller redirects stdout/stderr;
621	# returns ssh's exit code.
622	zbujb_workload_ssh() {

626	  local -r z_remote_cmd="$*"
627	
628	  ssh -i "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"       \
629	      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"          \
630	      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"   \
631	      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}" \
632	      "${BUJB_workload_user}@${BURN_HOST}" \
633	      "${z_remote_cmd}"
634	}

file.content:
test $# -eq 1 \
    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_exec_*: requires exactly one statement (got $#); decompose multi-statement work via Capture-Decide-Dispatch per WSG SH-10"
  local -r z_body="$1"

  # Escape " → \" for the cmd.exe / Windows argv-parser layer.
  local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"        \
      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"             \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"      \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}"  \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"  \
      "${z_remote_invoker} -c \"${z_body_escaped}\""
}

# zbujb_admin_powershell BODY... -- run a PowerShell statement chain on
# the remote node as the privileged user. Prepends discipline prelude:
# $ErrorActionPreference='Stop' (PS errors terminate), $env:WSL_UTF8=1
# (wsl.exe emits UTF-8), $LASTEXITCODE=0 (initialize so the trailing
# if-check doesn't trip on the $null default — $null -ne 0 evaluates True
# in PowerShell typed comparison, which would fire `exit $null` and
# discard buffered object-formatter output for cmdlets like Get-LocalUser
# whose tables haven't flushed to stdout yet). Trailing if-check
# propagates $LASTEXITCODE on non-zero from native commands in the body.
# Caller redirects stdout/stderr; returns ssh's exit code.
zbujb_admin_powershell() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test $# -ge 1 \
    || buc_die "zbujb_admin_powershell: PowerShell command body required"
  local -r z_body="$*"
  local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"        \
      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"             \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"      \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}"  \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"  \
      "${BUJB_ps_invoke_command} \"${BUJB_ps_prelude} ${z_body_escaped}; if (\$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { exit \$LASTEXITCODE }\""
}

# zbujb_powershell_capture ROLE BODY -- run a single PS expression on the
# remote node as the named role and emit its stdout. Windows CR is stripped
# via bash parameter expansion (no subprocess) so $(...) yields a clean
# value. Single-expression discipline per WSG-PS-5; multi-statement bodies
# do not belong here. Caller || buc_die on capture failure.
# ROLE: zbujb_privileged | zbujb_workload
zbujb_powershell_capture() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test $# -ge 2 \
    || buc_die "zbujb_powershell_capture: ROLE BODY required"
  local -r z_role="$1"; shift
  local -r z_body="$*"
  local -r z_body_escaped="${z_body//\"/\\\"}"   # WSp-109

  local z_key z_user
  case "${z_role}" in
    zbujb_privileged) z_key="${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"; z_user="${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}" ;;
    zbujb_workload)   z_key="${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}";   z_user="${BUJB_workload_user}"   ;;
    *)                buc_die "zbujb_powershell_capture: unknown role '${z_role}' (zbujb_privileged|zbujb_workload)" ;;
  esac

  local z_out z_exit=0
  z_out=$(ssh -i "${z_key}"                       \
              "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"         \
              -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"  \
              -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}" \
              "${z_user}@${BURN_HOST}"            \
              "${BUJB_ps_invoke_command} \"${BUJB_ps_prelude} ${z_body_escaped}\"") \
    || z_exit=$?

  printf '%s' "${z_out//$'\r'/}"
  return "${z_exit}"
}

# zbujb_workload_ssh REMOTE_CMD -- execute a Windows-native command line
# as the workload user via SSH. The default Windows OpenSSH shell is
# cmd.exe; the command line goes through cmd.exe's argv layer to the named
# binary. Used by garrison-w for `wsl.exe --import` (which writes to the
# workload's HKCU\Lxss because the SSH session is a real workload logon)
# and `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -c "..."`
# invocations that follow the import. Caller redirects stdout/stderr;
# returns ssh's exit code.
zbujb_workload_ssh() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test $# -ge 1 \
    || buc_die "zbujb_workload_ssh: REMOTE_CMD required"
  local -r z_remote_cmd="$*"

  ssh -i "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"       \
      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"          \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"   \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}" \
      "${BUJB_workload_user}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      "${z_remote_cmd}"
}

# SSH option values used inline at every ssh call alongside
# ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS. BatchMode=yes / ConnectTimeout=15 is the dominant
# pair; caparison-windows phase 1's password-fallback case uses
# BatchMode=no inline and step6_validate's knock uses ConnectTimeout=10
# inline.
BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes='BatchMode=yes'
BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15='ConnectTimeout=15'

# PowerShell CLI invocation forms. _command for one-shot string invocations
# (the dominant case); _file_stdin for streaming a PS script body from
# stdin (caparison-windows phases 1 and 2).
BUJB_ps_invoke_command='powershell -NoProfile -Command'
BUJB_ps_invoke_file_stdin='powershell -NoProfile -File -'

# PowerShell session discipline prelude shared by admin_powershell and
# powershell_capture:
#   $ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'  — PS errors terminate the session.
#   $env:WSL_UTF8 = 1                — wsl.exe emits UTF-8 (not UTF-16).
#   $LASTEXITCODE = 0                — initialize so a trailing if-check
#     does not trip on the $null default ($null -ne 0 evaluates True in
#     PowerShell typed comparison, which would fire `exit $null` and
#     discard buffered object-formatter output for cmdlets like
#     Get-LocalUser whose tables have not flushed to stdout yet).
BUJB_ps_prelude="\$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'; \$env:WSL_UTF8 = 1; \$LASTEXITCODE = 0;"

# PowerShell-form path to the Windows OpenSSH sshd_config. Bash-side
# substitution at the use site expands this tinder, baking the literal
# $env:ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config into the constructed PS body.
BUJB_ps_sshd_config_path='$env:ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config'

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zbujb_*)

zbujb_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZBUJB_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module bujb already kindled"

  # Per-call captures for caparison (autonumbered). Each ssh call gets
  # numbered stdout/stderr at ${PREFIX}${idx}_${label}_stdout.txt. Most
  # callsites route through zbujb_caparison_run; caparison-windows phase
  # 1/2 callers bump the counter and build paths locally because
  # verify_directives must re-read phase 1's stdout after the call.
  readonly ZBUJB_CAPARISON_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_caparison_"

  # stat captures (per-key-slot for parallel forensics on resolve).
  readonly ZBUJB_STAT_STDOUT_PRIV="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_stat_priv_stdout.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_STAT_STDERR_PRIV="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_stat_priv_stderr.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_STAT_STDOUT_WORK="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_stat_work_stdout.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_STAT_STDERR_WORK="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_stat_work_stderr.txt"

  # ssh-keygen dry-load captures (resolve-time, per slot).
  readonly ZBUJB_DRYLOAD_STDOUT_PRIV="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_dryload_priv_stdout.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_DRYLOAD_STDERR_PRIV="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_dryload_priv_stderr.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_DRYLOAD_STDOUT_WORK="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_dryload_work_stdout.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_DRYLOAD_STDERR_WORK="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_dryload_work_stderr.txt"

  # ssh-keygen pubkey-emit captures (garrison step4 = workload pubkey,
  # caparison-windows phase1 = admin pubkey).
  readonly ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDOUT_WORK="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_pubkey_work_stdout.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDERR_WORK="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_pubkey_work_stderr.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDOUT_PRIV="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_pubkey_priv_stdout.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_PUBKEY_STDERR_PRIV="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_pubkey_priv_stderr.txt"

  # base64 capture (garrison step4 w-branch = authorized_keys line b64-encoded
  # for transport to remote).
  readonly ZBUJB_AUTHKEYS_B64_STDOUT="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_authkeys_b64_stdout.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_AUTHKEYS_B64_STDERR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_authkeys_b64_stderr.txt"

  # Obliterate per-call captures (reused across the Windows sub-step
  # sequence — last call's content is what's preserved at die time).
  readonly ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDOUT="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_obliterate_stdout.txt"
  readonly ZBUJB_OBLITERATE_STDERR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_obliterate_stderr.txt"

  # Output capture paths for bujb_command_file (workload stdout/stderr/exit).
  readonly ZBUJB_OUTPUT_STDOUT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/stdout.log"
  readonly ZBUJB_OUTPUT_STDERR="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/stderr.log"
  readonly ZBUJB_OUTPUT_EXITCODE="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/exitcode"

  # Per-call captures for zbujb_garrison_step4_place_trust.
  readonly ZBUJB_PLACE_TRUST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_place_trust_"

  # Per-call captures for zbujb_garrison_step6_validate.
  readonly ZBUJB_VALIDATE_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_validate_"

  # Per-call captures for the garrison-w SSH-as-workload phases
  # (export-seed, init-wsl, lockdown, seed-cleanup).
  readonly ZBUJB_W_INIT_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/bujb_w_init_"

  # Per-call forensic capture for obliterate sub-steps (counter pattern,
  # like the other _run wrappers). Each ssh call lands at a uniquely
  # numbered file, so probe and destructive ops both leave evidence.
